title: Hearing Protection Act
plain_summary: This bill would make it easier to buy firearm silencers by treating them more like regular guns instead of heavily controlled weapons. The government would have to destroy all the records it currently keeps about who owns silencers. States would not be allowed to make special taxes or registration rules for silencers. Instead, there would be a ten percent federal tax when people buy them.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provis

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Hearing Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Equal Treatment of Silencers and Firearms
sections.text: This section changes the tax law to remove silencers (devices that reduce the sound when a gun is fired) from the special list of heavily regulated firearms. Currently, silencers are treated the same as machine guns and other heavily restricted weapons under federal tax law. This change removes silencers from that special category. The new rule will start ninety days after this law is passed, beginning with the first full three-month period after that date.

sections.title: Section 3  Treatment of Certain Silencers
sections.text: This section adds a new rule to the tax law. If someone legally gets or owns a firearm silencer under regular federal gun laws, they will automatically be considered to have met all the registration and licensing requirements of the National Firearms Act for that silencer. The National Firearms Act is the federal law that heavily regulates certain types of weapons and requires special paperwork and fees.

sections.title: Section 4  Preemption of Certain State Laws in Relation to Firearm Silencers
sections.text: This section changes federal gun law to override certain state and local laws about firearm silencers. State and local governments cannot impose special taxes on making, transferring, using, owning, or transporting firearm silencers when those activities involve commerce between states or with other countries. States also cannot require special markings, record keeping, or registration for such silencers. Regular sales taxes that apply to all products are still allowed.

sections.title: Section 5  Destruction of Records
sections.text: Within one year after this law is passed, the Attorney General must destroy all government records related to silencers. This includes any silencer registrations kept in the National Firearms Registration and Transfer Record, any applications to transfer silencers that show who received them, and any applications to make silencers that show who made them.

sections.title: Section 6  Amendments to Title 18, United States Code
sections.text: This section updates the main federal criminal law to better define firearm silencers and firearm mufflers. It explains that these devices are designed to reduce the sound of a portable gun and can be attached directly to the gun or through mounting equipment. The definition also covers the main housing or structure that holds the sound-reducing parts. The section also updates laws about selling guns to include silencers and mufflers, so the same age restrictions and other rules apply. Finally, it creates new rules for how manufacturers and importers must put serial numbers on silencers and mufflers, usually on the main outer part, unless the Attorney General approves marking it somewhere else.

sections.title: Section 7  Imposition of Tax on Firearm Silencers or Firearm Mufflers
sections.text: This section adds firearm silencers and firearm mufflers to the list of items that have a ten percent tax when manufacturers sell them. The section defines these devices as anything made to silence, muffle, or reduce the sound of a portable gun. It also updates related tax rules to include these devices alongside other firearms and ammunition. These tax changes will start ninety days after this law is passed, beginning with the first full three-month period after that date.
